content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. Selection reform is now live government policy with EU tailwind but faces low-probability cross-party durability, high reversal risk on government turnover, and non-negotiable stakeholder preconditions (workforce guarantees, statutory funding) that the minister has not yet addressed, making it politically feasible in the medium term (2027–2028) only if the government narrows its scope to pilot-phase rebalancing coupled with explicit quality-levelling commitments, abandons abolition rhetoric, and negotiates binding funding guarantees—the opposite of which suggests either stalled-indefinite consultation or legislative reversal within one electoral cycle.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: The Tisza Party's 12 April 2026 electoral victory (141 of 199 seats, ~53% party-list) created a constitutional two-thirds mandate; education received a separate ministry for the first time since 2010, signalling structural reform is now institutionally feasible.

Education Minister Lannert Judit (June 2026) publicly stated that 6/8-year gimnázium students do not develop faster than age-cohort peers; the system's only function is elite self-selection and socioeconomic status reproduction, not pedagogy. She framed the question as a societal-policy choice, not technical, and explicitly committed to review rather than abolition, with current gimnáziums to retain their position.
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: Will the government publish a formal legislative timetable, Oktatási Hivatal review mandate, or government resolution on selection reform, moving it beyond consultative intent?

Are statutory (vs annual-budget) funding-floor guarantees politically achievable under the projected fiscal consolidation pressures, or will compensatory funding remain contingent on annual budget cycles?
